LZPM-TCT: Born in 1590 at Bowridge Hill, Dorset, John Greene was raised in the Anglican faith of Wiltshire, England, before departing aboard the HMS James of London in April 1635 and arriving at Boston that June. Drawn to the religious dissenter Roger Williams, he became one of eleven men baptized as original members of the First Baptist Church in America, helped purchase and settle Providence, Rhode Island, and was among the twelve to whom Williams deeded land bought from the Narragansett sachems. His convictions cost him dearly: fined and banished from Massachusetts jurisdiction in 1637, and his second wife killed by Massachusetts raiders in the 1643 persecution of the Shawomet pioneers. As one of the twelve original founders of Warwick, he served his colony for decades as commissioner, magistrate, town councilman, and assembly representative until his death there in 1659.
LZGX-VLX: Born in Salisbury, Wiltshire, in 1620, Major John Greene came to New England as a young man, possibly aboard the James of London that carried his father in 1635. He rose to become one of Rhode Island's most prominent figures, named in the colony's 1663 royal charter and commissioned a militia captain the following year. Promoted repeatedly to 'Major of the Main,' the highest military rank in the colony, he commanded all the mainland trained bands, introduced Rhode Island to privateering, and helped establish the Boston Post Road and a post office. He also served as Deputy Governor of Rhode Island through the 1690s, raising his family at Occupasuetaxet before his death in Warwick in 1708.
LR7T-FWX: Born in London in 1603 and educated at Cambridge, where he graduated from Pembroke College, Roger Williams was a brilliant minister who spoke at least seven languages, including Narragansett. He arrived at Boston in 1631 aboard the ship Lyon but his outspoken views on religious liberty and the separation of church and state led the Massachusetts General Court to banish him in 1635 for 'new and dangerous opinions.' Escaping into the wilderness in January 1636 with the help of four companions, he founded Providence Plantations, the first permanent settlement in Rhode Island, built on a covenant of freedom of worship, and established the First Baptist Church in America. A noted author of A Key into the Language of America and The Bloudy Tenent of Persecution, he died in Providence in 1683 and has since been reinterred three times, finally beneath his memorial statue at Prospect Terrace.
LYNX-9NC: Born in 1583 at Barton St David, Somerset, Henry Adams was named co-executor of his father's estate alongside his mother and lived in Somerset for many years before emigrating to New England. He sailed on the ship Mary & John, arriving in Massachusetts with his wife and large family of sons and a daughter, and in 1641 received a 40-acre land grant at 'The Mount' in what became Braintree. There he worked as a farmer, maltster, and yeoman, also serving his community as constable, until his death in 1646. He is remembered as the great-grandfather of President John Adams and founding father Samuel Adams.
93B9-5CB: Born in Pitminster, Somerset, in 1622, Robert Treat emigrated to America around age eight with his parents and eight siblings, settling first in Dorchester before the family moved on to Wethersfield and then Milford in the New Haven Colony. Still a teenager, he was called upon to help plan and lay out the original village of Milford, beginning a lifetime of public leadership. Dissatisfied with the 1665 union of the New Haven and Connecticut colonies, he led a group of Milford settlers to found Newark, New Jersey, before returning home, and he served as a militia officer in King Philip's War. He went on to become Deputy Governor and then Governor of Connecticut, securing a protective patent for Milford's land titles, and died in Milford in 1710.
LZ61-FG5: Born in Virginia in 1654, Colonel Thomas Ballard Jr. lived on a plantation in Yorktown, part of whose land was later sold to help found the College of William and Mary. He served as sheriff of York County in 1694 and held the rank of lieutenant colonel of the militia. He died in 1711 and was buried in the cemetery of Bruton Parish Church in Williamsburg.
L6LN-RDY: Born in Maidstone, Kent, in 1623, Caleb Carr crossed the Atlantic aboard the Elizabeth Ann in 1635 and settled in Newport, Rhode Island. He devoted some four decades to public service in the colony, serving as a Justice of the Court of Quarter Sessions in the late 1680s. In May 1695 he was elected Governor of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations, but held the office only a few months before his death in Newport that December.
9MTJ-YTD: Born in Norwich, Norfolk, in 1599, William Towne worked as a basket maker and gardener before emigrating to New England and arriving at Salem in 1635. He received a land grant near his Salem home in 1640 and later acquired a 40-acre farm in Topsfield, where he lived until his death in 1673.
LYFC-R9P: Born in 1678 near Ludwigshafen in the German Rhineland-Palatinate, Josephus Johann Brunner worked as a farmer and served as a godfather in his home region before emigrating. He sailed from Rotterdam and arrived at Philadelphia aboard the ship Allen in September 1729. He eventually settled at Schifferstadt near Frederick, Maryland, where he died in 1753.
L4N5-C86: Born in Worcestershire, England, around 1630, Thomas Ballard Sr. built a distinguished public career in colonial Virginia, beginning as a county clerk in 1652 and rising to High Sheriff of James City County. He served as a Burgess and ultimately as Speaker of the Virginia House of Burgesses from 1680 to 1682, while also acting as a Justice of the Peace and holding the militia rank of colonel. He died in James City, Virginia, in 1690.
LY8N-KY1: Born in Scotland around 1639, Daniel Davison endured a period of captivity as a prisoner in the early 1650s before immigrating to the Americas in 1657. He settled in the Ipswich area of Essex County, Massachusetts, where he lived until his death in 1693.
LZP8-QFN: Born in 1631 at Weyhill, Hampshire, Agnes Bent was a granddaughter of Robert Bent and daughter of John Bent. She emigrated from Southampton in the spring of 1638 and arrived at Watertown in the Massachusetts Bay Colony that summer. She married Edward Rice, was named in her father's will as Agnes Rice, and lived until 1713 in Marlborough, Massachusetts.
MP9Q-CCB: Born in Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1655, Tabitha Stone spent her life in the same community where she was born. In September 1697 she was taken captive by Indians at Lancaster, an ordeal she survived. She lived in Sudbury until her death there in 1719.
LZ2Y-D2J: Born in 1595 at Caister-on-Sea, Norfolk, Joanna Blessing was a Puritan housewife who emigrated to New England and arrived at Salem around 1635. She joined the Salem church in 1648 and lived until 1682, dying in Topsfield in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
LZ8Y-98N: Born in 1637 at Clapham in Yorkshire and baptized there, Robert Heaton IV was a Quaker who received his removal from the Settle Monthly Meeting in 1682 to emigrate to Pennsylvania. He sailed aboard 'The Lamb' that October and in 1686 took up 250 acres of land in Middletown Township, Bucks County. He lived there until his death in 1717 and was buried in the Middletown Friends Cemetery.
L1W2-FV7: Born in Kingweston, Somerset, in 1626, Captain Joseph Adams Sr. emigrated to New England in 1638, settling at Mount Wollaston in what became Braintree, Massachusetts. He made his living as a farmer, maltster, and millwright, and served his town as constable while holding the militia rank of captain. He died in Braintree in 1694.
L13M-MGS: Born in East Farleigh, Kent, in 1605 and styled a Baron of the Exchequer, Captain Jeremiah Clarke arrived at Boston in 1630 and later helped incorporate Newport, Rhode Island, in 1639. He served Newport as constable and rose through the militia from lieutenant to captain, while holding the office of treasurer for Portsmouth and Providence. In 1648 he served as Governor of Rhode Island, and he died at Newport in 1652.
M851-RCD: Born in 1614 at Aber-Bran in Breconshire, Wales, Daniel Gaines arrived in colonial Virginia around 1650 and patented 400 acres of land in Rappahannock County in 1663. He served as a captain in the colonial Virginia militia and became the first Justice of the Peace for Old Rappahannock County, where he lived until his death in 1684.
LT3J-BXN: Born in Chichester, Sussex, in 1612, John Heard was a sea captain who plied the Atlantic from England before settling in New England. He immigrated to the Piscataqua plantation at Kittery in 1634, possibly aboard the Warwick, and by 1636 had made his home in Dover, New Hampshire, where he lived until his death in 1689.
MT74-SJW: Born in England in 1609, Frances Latham immigrated to Newport, Rhode Island, in 1637. She lived there until her death in 1677 and was buried in the Newport Cemetery, where her gravestone identifies her as 'Mrs. Frances Vaughan.'
LZ8Y-9W2: Born in 1660 at Giggleswick in Yorkshire, England, Thomas Stackhouse settled in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he served as a Colonial Assembly representative. He died in 1744 at Langhorne in Middletown Township and was buried in the Middletown Friends Cemetery.
LZSS-S4L: Born at Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1637, Sarah Towne lived through one of colonial New England's darkest episodes. In 1692, during the Salem Witch Trials, she was accused of witchcraft but escaped execution. Known later as Sarah Towne Clayes, she lived until 1703 and died in Framingham, Massachusetts.
LZNK-H1Z: John Rice was born in 1651 in Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony and lived his entire life there, dying in the same town in 1719.
LYB6-GG9: Born in 1643 at Annapolis in colonial Maryland, John Hammond rose to high military command in his home colony. He held the rank of Major General over all the Maryland militia except the Eastern Shore, including Kent Island. He died in Anne Arundel County, Maryland, in 1707.
LY12-4BV: Born in 1629 at Brielle in Holland, Jan Corneliszen Buys immigrated to New Amsterdam in 1648 and worked as a soldier. Granted 40 acres at Bergen, he was driven from his home by Indian raiders in the mid-1650s, after which he was granted permission to operate a tavern in New Amsterdam. He later obtained a land patent on Long Island and settled in the Bushwick and Brooklyn area, then known as Breuckelen, where he lived until his death in 1690.
GQHP-26N: Born in Cumberland, England, in 1655, Sir Edward Radclyffe became the 2nd Earl of Derwentwater in 1697 and also held the titles of 4th Baronet of Dilston, Viscount Langley, Viscount Radclyffe, and Baron Tyndale. He separated from his wife in 1700 and died at Westminster in 1705. His remains were later reinterred in October 1874 at St Mary's Catholic Cemetery in Hexham, Northumberland.
KNXX-B1H: Born in 1622 at Stanstead, Suffolk, England, Edward Rice arrived at Boston in 1639 and soon removed to Sudbury, later relocating to Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1664. He was mentioned as a brother-in-law in the will of Peter Bent, and in 1687 his community honored him by electing him a deacon. He lived in Marlborough until his death in 1712.
LCZR-WSL: Born in 1658 in Old Rappahannock, Virginia, Colonel John Catlett Jr. devoted his life to public service in the colony. He served as a Justice of the Peace and a Justice of the County Court, was repeatedly elected to the Virginia House of Burgesses between 1693 and 1702, and held the offices of coroner and sheriff of Essex County. He acquired substantial land, including an 817-acre patent in 1704, acted as a town trustee selling lots in the Burgh of Rappahannock, and held the militia rank of colonel before his death in Essex County in 1724.
